coredata.dc:title: Label-free optical and electrical immunoassays based on lyotropic chromonic liquid crystals: Implications of real-time detection and kinetic analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Shaban, Hassanein
Hsieh, Jui-Teng
Lee, Mon-Juan
Lee, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Conventional liquid crystal (LC)-based biosensors utilize predominantly thermotropic LCs as the signal-transducing media, which are less environmentally sustainable compared with lyotropic counterparts. In this study, the nematic phase of the anionic azo dye sunset yellow (SSY), a type of lyotropic chromonic liquid crystals (LCLCs), was employed in the optical and electrical biosensing of bovine serum albumin (BSA) and the cancer biomarker CA125. The optical response observed under a polarizing optical microscope was quantified by image analysis, taking advantage of the specific absorption of SSY. The electrical response derived from the dielectric spectra of SSY provided a new alternative for quantitative bioassay based on nematic LCLCs. The limit of detection (LOD) of the optical and electrical protein assay was ∼10−11- and ∼10−10-g/ml BSA, respectively, whereas that of the optical and electrical immunoassay was 5.97 × 10−11 and 6.02 × 10−12 g/ml for CA125, respectively. Moreover, real-time monitoring and kinetic analysis, which are hardly achievable for the hydrophobic thermotropic LCs, were demonstrated by dispersing CA125 in nematic SSY and subsequently recording the optical response over time during the specific binding between CA125 and the immobilized anti-CA125 antibody. Results from this study further the potential of nematic LCLCs in biosensing, especially in dielectric and real-time detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lyotropic chromonic liquid crystal
Azo dye
Sunset yellow
Label-free immunosensor
Real-time monitoring
Dielectric spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nematic SSY-based real-time monitoring and kinetic analysis in CA125 immunoassay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Working principle of the LCLC bioassay on the basis of vertically aligned SSY assemblages. (a) The chemical structure of SSY and the formation of columnar aggregates of SSY molecules with increasing concentrations. (b) DMOAP-induced vertical alignment of SSY stacks and the corresponding dark optical texture. (c) Disruption of aligned nematic SSY by biomolecules atop the glass surface and the resulting light leakage.

Nematic SSY-based optical (i) protein assay and (ii) CA125 immunoassay. (a,d) Optical textures of nematic SSY in the presence of various concentrations of BSA or CA125. (b) Conoscopic images of nematic SSY in the presence of various concentrations of BSA. (c,e) Image analysis of the optical textures as shown in (a) by calculating separately the relative intensity of the R-, G-, B-components of the POM images as well as the average intensity of the three primary-color components. The straight lines represent the results of linear regression of the correlation between relative intensity and the logarithm of BSA or CA125 concentration.

Nematic SSY-based dielectric (i) protein assay and (ii) CA125 immunoassay. (a,c,e,g) The dielectric spectra of the real and imaginary part of the dielectric constant, ε′(f) and ε”(f), over the frequency range of 1 Hz–100 kHz at 0–10−5 g/ml BSA or CA125. (b,d,f,h) Correlation of the relative percent difference, Δε’/ε′(0) and
Δ
ε max ″
/
ε max ″
(0), to the logarithm of BSA concentration. The dotted lines represent the results of linear and 2nd-degree polynomial regression of the correlation between the derived electrical parameters and the logarithm of CA125 concentration.

Comparison of CA125 detection sensitivity between nematic SSY-based optical and dielectric biosensing within the linear dynamic range of selected calibration curves acquired from Figs. 2 and 3. The relative response ratio, r/r p, was calculated by dividing the relative intensity of the R-component or the average intensity of the three color components in Fig. 2(e), and the relative percent difference, Δε’/ε′(0)% or Δ
ε max ″
/
ε max ″
(0)%, in Fig. 3(f) and (h), respectively, by the corresponding values at 10−12 g/ml CA125. The dashed lines represent the results of linear regression of the correlation between the r/r p ratio and the logarithm of CA125 concentration.

Nematic SSY-based real-time monitoring and kinetic analysis in CA125 immunoassay. (a) Real-time detection and binding kinetics of CA125 antigen‒antibody immunoreaction. The curve fitting results are shown as noncontinuous lines. (b) Effect of reaction time on detection sensitivity. CA125 calibration curves derived from (a) at three selected binding intervals, 50, 500, and 900 min are compared. The dashed lines represent the results of linear regression of the correlation between ΔI(t)% and the logarithm of CA125 concentration. (c) Variation of LOD with the reaction time. The LOD values determined from the CA125 calibration curves at selected time points from (a) are compared.
Comparison of the range of detection (ROD) and the limit of detection (LOD) of CA125 immunodetection techniques.

head.ce:title: Label-free optical and electrical immunoassays based on lyotropic chromonic liquid crystals: Implications of real-time detection and kinetic analysis

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Medical Science Industries, Chang Jung Christian University, Guiren Dist., Tainan, 711301, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional liquid crystal (LC)-based biosensors utilize predominantly thermotropic LCs as the signal-transducing media, which are less environmentally sustainable compared with lyotropic counterparts. In this study, the nematic phase of the anionic azo dye sunset yellow (SSY), a type of lyotropic chromonic liquid crystals (LCLCs), was employed in the optical and electrical biosensing of bovine serum albumin (BSA) and the cancer biomarker CA125. The optical response observed under a polarizing optical microscope was quantified by image analysis, taking advantage of the specific absorption of SSY. The electrical response derived from the dielectric spectra of SSY provided a new alternative for quantitative bioassay based on nematic LCLCs. The limit of detection (LOD) of the optical and electrical protein assay was ∼10−11- and ∼10−10-g/ml BSA, respectively, whereas that of the optical and electrical immunoassay was 5.97 × 10−11 and 6.02 × 10−12 g/ml for CA125, respectively. Moreover, real-time monitoring and kinetic analysis, which are hardly achievable for the hydrophobic thermotropic LCs, were demonstrated by dispersing CA125 in nematic SSY and subsequently recording the optical response over time during the specific binding between CA125 and the immobilized anti-CA125 antibody. Results from this study further the potential of nematic LCLCs in biosensing, especially in dielectric and real-time detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analysed during this study are included in the manuscript and the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:para: Lyotropic liquid crystals (LLCs) are concentration- and temperature-dependent mesogenic phase formed when amphiphilic molecules, containing a hydrophobic nonpolar aliphatic chain combined with a hydrophilic polar head group, are dissolved in aqueous or nonaqueous solvents, or when nonamphiphilic molecules such as certain planar molecules of chromonic dyes and drugs are mixed with a polar solvent, typically water, forming sufficiently long stacks to yield lyotropic chromonic LC (LCLC) phases (Luk et al., 2005). In comparison with biosensing involving thermotropic LCs, lyotropic LC-based biosensing is relatively less explored. The aqueous solution of disodium cromoglycate (DSCG) as a LCLC provides a nontoxic medium to preserve the activity of vesicular stomatitis virus and its replication in human cervical epithelial carcinoma cells (Cheng et al., 2005), and enables the motility of bacteria to be monitored (Dhakal et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2013; Mushenheim et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2017). Our previous report suggests that planarly aligned nematic DSCG—14-wt% DSCG in water—can be used in optical detection and spectrometric quantitation of the protein standard bovine serum albumin (BSA) as well as the cancer biomarker CA125 (Shaban et al., 2021a).

In this study, the disodium salt of 6-hydroxy-5-[(4-sulfophenyl)azo]-2-naphthalene sulfonic acid, an anionic azo dye known as sunset yellow (SSY, Fig. 1 (a)), was applied as the sensing medium of quantitative LCLC-based optical and electrical bioassays for BSA and CA125. Exhibiting optical absorption at ∼482 nm, SSY is an orange food coloring commonly used to improve the aesthetics of food products (Park et al., 2011). In an aqueous solution, the sodium ion (Na+) dissociates, and the resulting negatively charged SSY anions form chromonic aggregates via π–π stacking of their polyaromatic cores, which are spaced by ∼0.33 nm (Lydon, 2010; Park et al., 2008). The nematic phase of SSY in water, comprising rod-like assemblages of the chromonic aggregates, is found within the concentration range of 28–34 wt% (Edwards et al., 2008). Planarly aligned SSY was employed in the selective detection of Legionella pneumophila (Otón et al., 2019), but the potential of SSY in biomolecular recognition has not been examined. Compared with DSCG, the birefringence of SSY is significantly higher, indicating superior detection sensitivity. At 20 °C, the absolute birefringence
|
Δ n
|
of nematic SSY is 0.06–0.11 over a concentration range of 0.94 M (29.8 wt%)–1.25 M (36.12 wt%) (Horowitz et al., 2005), whereas that of nematic DSCG is 0.014–0.026 within a concentration range of 12–17 wt% (McGinn et al., 2013; Nastishin et al., 2005). It was reported that the anchoring strength W (in J⋅m−2) for the homeotropic alignment of SSY is stronger than that of DSCG (Jeong et al., 2014), leading to a decrease in the extrapolation length b (≡ K/W), where K is the distortion elastic constant ranging from 1 to 10 pN (Popov et al., 2017). This implies that analytes of low molecular weights or concentrations may induce a more significant change in stack orientation of SSY than DSCG. Moreover, the temperature range of the nematic phase of SSY, 20–70 °C (Park et al., 2008), is much broader than that of DSCG (20–40 °C) (Aranson, 2018; Hartshorne and Woodard, 1981), ensuring better thermal stability and, in turn, more reliable quantitation.

A sandwich-like LC‒glass biodetection platform based on homeotropically aligned SSY was constructed by modifying the glass surface with the vertical-alignment agent dimethyloctadecyl[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ammonium chloride (DMOAP) (Fig. 1(b)). When the vertical anchoring strength stemming from such a DMOAP coating is attenuated by biological analytes immobilized at the LC‒glass interface, the optical signal induced by the reorientation of nematic SSY in the form of light leakage can be observed under a polarizing optical microscope (POM) as in the case of thermotropic LC-based biosensors (Fig. 1(c)). Furthermore, because the nematic phase of SSY allows the electrical properties to be analyzed similarly to thermotropic nematic LCs, detection and quantitative analysis relying on the dielectric features of the SSY aggregates were performed through dielectric spectroscopy. Finally, taking advantage of the water solubility of biomolecules in lyotropic LCs, real-time detection and kinetic analysis were accomplished by mixing CA125 with SSY in water and monitoring the increase in light leakage over time as binding between CA125 and the immobilized anti-CA125 antibody occurred. As current label-based CA125 immunoassays require fluorescence-labeled antibodies and fluorescence spectrophotometry to transduce and process the optical signal (Bast et al., 1981), results from this study offer an alternative biosensing platform to reduce the cost and increase the feasibility of point-of-care cancer screening.
Please refer to Section S1 of Supplementary Information.

The principle of LCLC-based optical biodetection and optimization of SSY concentration were explained in sections S2 and S3 of Supplementary Information. BSA was immobilized at various concentrations on the DMOAP-coated glass substrate, and a sandwich-like cell having a cell gap of ∼15 μm was filled with 28 wt% of SSY solution. Without BSA, the completely dark optical texture suggested that the SSY assemblages are vertically aligned (Fig. 2 (a)). As the BSA concentration was elevated from 10−12 to 10−5 g/ml, the brightness of the optical texture was enhanced, indicating that the SSY aggregates are tilted away from vertical alignment. The gradual decrease in the tilt angle of the vertically aligned SSY aggregates with increasing BSA concentration was evident from the result of conoscopic measurements as depicted in Fig. 2(b). When the SSY aggregates were aligned vertically at 0-g/ml BSA, the conoscopic interference pattern was characterized by the Maltese cross, which represents the coincidence of the vibration direction of light with the transmission axis of either the polarizer or the analyzer. By introducing BSA at the LC‒glass interface to arouse random azimuthal angles of the disrupted orientation of SSY columnar assemblages, the contrast of the conoscopic interference pattern decreased at 10−12-g/ml BSA, and deformed to become unrecognizable at higher BSA concentrations of 10−11 and 10−10 g/ml.

The POM images in Fig. 2(a) were subjected to image analysis to obtain the correlation between the relative intensity of the optical texture and BSA concentration (Fig. 2(c)). The relative intensity of the individual R-, G-, and B-components, as well as the average intensity of the three primary-color components, increased with increasing BSA concentration. In particular, the R-component exhibited greater growth in relative intensity with increasing BSA concentration because of the specific absorption of SSY at around 482 nm (Park et al., 2011), as confirmed by the greater slope of the linear regression line, an indication of better detection sensitivity. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated in accordance with the equation
(1)
LOD = 3 s / m ,
where s represents the standard deviation of the y-intercept, and m is the slope of the linear regression line (Shrivastava and Gupta, 2011). The LOD values were 1.98 × 10−11, 7.31 × 10−11, 8.37 × 10−11, and 1.77 × 10−11 g/ml-BSA as deduced from the linear regression in Fig. 2(c) of the relative intensity of the R-, G-, B-components, and the average of the three components, respectively. Compared with our previously reported nematic DSCG-based protein assay, in which an LOD of ∼10−10 g/ml-BSA was obtained through spectrometric analysis (Shaban et al., 2021a), optical detection with nematic SSY offered a lower LOD when the relative intensity of the R-component or the average intensity of the three color components was analyzed.

To distinguish optical signals generated by the CA125 antigen‒antibody complex from the antibody alone in the absence of CA125, the amount of the immobilized anti-CA125 antibody was limited to below the LOD, which was determined to be ranged from 3.36 to 4.8 × 10−11 g/ml (Section S4 of Supplementary Information), such that the homeotropic alignment of SSY was not affected. In the nematic SSY-based optical CA125 immunoassay, 10−12 g/ml of the anti-CA125 antibody was immobilized in the form of a 3 × 3 array at ∼2 fg/spot (Fig. S1), which was reacted with various concentrations of CA125 over the entire surface of the DMOAP-modified glass substrate for an optimized immunoreaction time of 30 min (Section S5 of Supplementary Information). After washing with DI water to remove unbound or nonspecifically bound CA125, light leakage observed within the circular area of antibody immobilization can be considered as optical signals resulting from the formation of the CA125 immunocomplex, while that detected outside the circular area indicates nonspecific binding of CA125 to DMOAP, which was significant only at higher CA125 concentrations (Fig. 2(d)). Selectivity of the immunoassay was also confirmed by applying BSA as the control analyte (Section S6 of Supplementary Information). By plotting the relative intensity of the R-, G-, and B-components as well as the average intensity of the POM images in Fig. 2(d) against the logarithm of CA125 concentration, linear correlations with high coefficient of determination, R 2 ∼ 0.96–0.98, were obtained (Fig. 2(e)). As expected, quantitative analysis according to the relative intensity of the R-component offered better detection sensitivity. The LOD of the nematic SSY-based CA125 immunodetection determined from Fig. 2(e) was in the range of 5.97–6.39 × 10−11 g/ml with a CA125 antibody immobilization concentration of 10−12 g/ml. Compared with nematic DSCG-based biosensing, for which the LOD determined by spectrophotometry was 10−10 g/ml of CA125 when 10−9-g/ml anti-CA125 antibody was immobilized (Shaban et al., 2021a), immunodetection with nematic SSY offered a similar LOD with three orders of magnitude less capture antibodies.

The switching of SSY aggregates from the twisted nematic state to vertical state in response to the external electric field can be attained at voltages greater than the threshold voltage, V th = 1.3–1.7 V (Li et al., 2022). Although the mesogenic unit of nematic SSY does not have an intrinsic collective dipole moment (Chami and Wilson, 2010), it could be electrically induced to exhibit positive dielectric anisotropy (Δε > 0) in that the dielectric constant parallel to the axis of the rod-like SSY aggregate (ε ||) is greater than that perpendicular to the axis (ε ⊥). In dielectric spectroscopy of LCs, the measured ε’ can be represented by the effective dielectric constant ε eff, given by
(2)
ε eff
=
ε ‖
sin 2
θ +
ε ⊥
cos 2
θ ,
where θ is the angle between the director (i.e., the averaged axis of the SSY aggregates in this study) and the plane of the glass substrate. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), ε′ in the lower-frequency range dropped with increasing BSA concentration, suggesting that the tilt angle of the SSY aggregates diminished gradually from 90°. In the spectra of ε”, on the other hand, the ionic contribution was observed at frequencies lower than 3 Hz, followed by a maximum at 13–17 Hz ascribed to the relaxation mode of the SSY aggregates (Fig. 3(c)). We further calculated the relative percent differences in ε′ at 1 Hz and
ε max ″
using the equations
(3)
Δ
ε ′
/
ε ′
( 0 )
=
(
ε ′
( 0 )
−
ε ′
( c )
ε ′
( 0 )
)
× 100 %

and
(4)
Δ
ε max ″
/
ε max ″
( 0 )
=
(
ε max ″
( 0 )
−
ε max ″
( c )
ε max ″
( 0 )
)
× 100 % ,
where Δε′ is the absolute difference between ε′ at 1 Hz at 0-g/ml BSA, ε′(0), and that in the presence of BSA, ε′(c), and
Δ
ε max ″
is the absolute difference between
ε max ″
at 0-g/ml BSA,
ε max ″
(0), and that in the presence of BSA,
ε max ″
(c). A positive and linear correlation was thus obtained when the relative percent differences in ε′ at 1 Hz and
ε max ″
were plotted against the logarithm of BSA concentration (Fig. 3(b) and (d)), giving rise to LOD values approximately on the order of 10−10 g/ml for BSA. It is clear from our results that protein quantitation can be realized through dielectric measurements of nematic LCLCs. In thermotropic nematic LCs, a lower LOD was usually achieved in dielectric measurements compared with optical detection (Lin et al., 2019; Shaban et al., 2021b). However, in the case of nematic LCLCs, the LOD determined by dielectric spectroscopic analysis was of the same order of magnitude as, or an order of magnitude higher than that in optical measurements. This may be explained by the interference caused by space charges to the dielectric response of LCLCs in the low-frequency range, rendering less significant signal amplification.

For dielectric immunoassay, the anti-CA125 antibody was immobilized on the entire electrode area of the ITO-coated glass substrate, followed by reacting with various concentrations of CA125. The ε′ and ε” spectra of nematic SSY were unaffected by the immobilized anti-CA125 antibody (data not shown), which supports the observation in optical texture that nematic SSY remained homeotropically aligned in the presence of 10−12-g/ml anti-CA125 antibody. After reaction with various concentrations of CA125, the vertical anchoring strength of DMOAP was attenuated and the orientation of SSY aggregates at the LCLC‒glass interface disturbed, resulting in ε′ and ε” spectra similar to those of BSA (Fig. 3(e) and (g) versus Fig. 3(a) and (c)). The relative percent differences in ε′ at 1 Hz and
ε max ″
, Δε’/ε′(0) and
Δ
ε max ″
/
ε max ″
(0), increased with increasing CA125 concentration (Fig. 3(f) and (h)). The LOD of CA125 determined by Δε’/ε’(0) and Δ
ε max ″
/
ε max ″
(0) were 7.05 × 10−11 and 6.02 × 10−12 g/ml, respectively, which were an order of magnitude lower than those determined by the optical methods (Fig. 2(d) and (e)). The comparable LOD values and range of detection (ROD) of nematic SSY-based biosensing to those of thermotropic LC-based (Table S1) and label-based methods (Table 1 ) provide compelling evidence that nematic SSY is a competent biosensing medium for transducing the signal of immunocomplex formation.

The optical and dielectric CA125 immunoassays based on nematic SSY were further compared by normalizing the optical or dielectric response at each CA125 concentration to that at 10−12-g/ml CA125, conducing to a relative response ratio designated r/r p, where the subscript p denotes the condition at the pico-scale concentration (Fig. 4 ). It is apparent from the slope of the linear regression line of the correlation between r/r p and CA125 concentration that optical methods relying on the relative intensity of the R-component or the average intensity of the three color components offered better detection sensitivity than the dielectric approach based on the relative percent difference, (Δε’/ε′(0)) × 100% or (Δε’’max/ε’’max(0)) × 100%. Moreover, as a potential point-of-care sensing technique, ITO conductive glass substrates were not required in the optical immunoassay, which further reduces the cost per assay. The visually observed optical signal may also be more comprehensive to the user compared to dielectric spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nematic SSY-based real-time monitoring and kinetic analysis in CA125 immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: Most LC‒glass biosensing techniques developed to date are end-point assays such as those reported in sections 3.2 and 3.4 for CA125 immunoassays, in which the antigen‒antibody complex is formed prior to signal detection. Thanks to the hydrophilic nature of LCLCs, real-time detection can be implemented by dissolving the analyte in the sensing medium and monitoring the time-evolved signal response. Due to the higher sensitivity of optical detection as shown in Fig. 4, a real-time optical immunoassay of CA125 was performed by immobilizing a 3 × 3 array of anti-CA125 antibodies at 10−12 g/ml, followed by assembling the LC cell and injecting with a mixture of 28-wt % SSY and various concentrations of CA125 (Fig. S2).

The optical texture of nematic SSY was recorded over time and the relative intensity at time t, I(t), was subtracted by that at 0 min, I(0), and divided by I(0) to obtain the relative percent difference in I(t), namely,
(5)
Δ I
( t )
% =
(
I
( t )
− I
( 0 )
I
( 0 )
)
× 100

as shown in Fig. 5 (a). As CA125 was reacted with the immobilized anti-CA125 antibody, the dimensionless ΔI(t)% increased with time, reaching a plateau after prolonged reaction, which enabled kinetic analysis to be performed as described in Section S8 of Supplementary Information. From the value of the time constant τ listed in Table S2, it is inferred that the time required for immunocomplex formation increased with decreasing CA125 concentration. The optimal time for the antigen‒antibody binding reaction was therefore investigated by analyzing the effect of reaction time on detection sensitivity. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the slopes of the calibration curves constructed by plotting ΔI(t)% at 500 and 900 min against CA125 concentration were comparable, but that at 50 min was significantly smaller. The LOD determined from the plot of ΔI(t)% versus CA125 concentration at various selected times also supported this observation (Fig. 5(c)). At t ≥ 500 min, the calculated LOD was within the order of magnitude of 10−10-g/ml CA125, but at t < 500 min, the LOD increased by an order of magnitude higher to ∼6 × 10−10 g/ml CA125 (Fig. 5(c)). It is therefore suggested that the time for the antigen‒antibody binding reaction can be shortened to 500 min without compromising detection sensitivity and LOD, which is still considered quite long in comparison with conventional antigen‒antibody binding assays. This is due in part to the relatively higher viscosity of the aqueous solution of SSY compared with water (Luk et al., 2005; Shiyanovskii et al., 2005), which reduced the rate of diffusion of an analyte in the nematic LCLC. This drawback can be improved by slightly increasing the temperature of the binding reaction to 37 °C to reduce the viscosity of the SSY solution so as to accelerate the diffusion of the analyte. Sonicating or stirring the CA125/SSY mixture during antigen‒antibody immunoreaction may also promote diffusion and shorten the reaction time.

Optical and electrical protein assay and immunoassay relying on the nematic phase of vertically aligned SSY aggregates as the optical and electrical transducer were proposed in this study. Results from the nematic SSY-based optical bioassays can be quantitatively analyzed through image analysis of the red component of the optical texture by virtue of the characteristic absorption of SSY at ∼482 nm. As for electrical bioassays, both the ε’ and ε” spectra of nematic SSY obtained from dielectric spectroscopy can be applied in the quantitative analysis of BSA and CA125. The LOD of the label-free biodetection reported in this study is comparable to those based on thermotropic LCs and label-based assays. In addition, the hydrophilic nature of SSY was utilized in real-time monitoring and kinetic analysis of the interaction between CA125 and the immobilized anti-CA125 antibody, which was unachievable for the nonpolar thermotropic LCs. To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first demonstration of dielectric biosensing and real-time detection at the LC‒glass interface using the nematic phase of LCLCs. Future point-of-care testing devices based on nematic SSY can be developed by integrating multi-channel microfluidic system with different cancer-related antibodies immobilized to facilitate multiplex immunodetection.

Hassanein Shaban: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Jui-Teng Hsieh: Investigation. Mon-Juan Lee: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Wei Lee: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan, under grant Nos. 109-2320-B-309-001, 110-2112-M-A49-023, and 110-2320-B-309-001.
